mappings.rationaleAI: Art as diverse cultural activities with contested definitions addresses diverse conceptions of the scope of art (A.1), while aesthetics examining creativity and interpretation connects to the philosophy of art (A) and the classification of arts (B).

The arts as an umbrella category encompassing visual, literary, and performing arts address diverse conceptions of scope (A.1) and classification of the arts (B), while cross-cultural artistic expression connects to characterization by cultural circumstances (B.3).
Aristotle's Poetics covering rhetoric and mimesis as artistic theory contributes to diverse theories concerning the nature, functions, and effects of art, specifically mimetic theories (A.2).

The history of art across cultures from prehistoric through contemporary periods addresses style in the arts (B.4), while its survey of cultural traditions connects to characterization by cultural circumstances (B.3).
Modernism's experimentation, abstraction, and self-conscious rejection of tradition address theories concerning the nature and effects of art (A.2) and period styles (B.4.b).
Romanticism's privileging of subjectivity, emotion, and imagination addresses expressive theories of art (A.2) and period style (B.4.b).
Realism's attempt to represent subject matter truthfully addresses mimetic theories of art (A.2) and constitutes a period style (B.4.b).
Picasso's co-founding of Cubism and stylistic reinvention addresses the creative process (A.3), while his multiple named periods exemplify personal and period styles (B.4.b).

Leonardo's systematic empirical curiosity spanning arts and sciences addresses the creative process (A.3), while his physical artistic production connects to the manipulation of physical matter (B.1.b).
Hokusai's expansion of ukiyo-e and catalysis of Japonisme addresses art characterized by cultural circumstances (B.3) and the nature of personal style (B.4.a).
Popular culture as art forms produced through mass media and capitalist mass appeal addresses the characterization of works by social circumstances and audience (B.3).
Folklore as expressive culture informally transmitted within communities addresses the primitive, folk, and popular arts (B.3).
Architecture as both visual art and applied engineering addresses art by reference to intention (useful and fine art) (B.1.a) and the manipulation of physical matter (B.1.b).
Design as the intentional creation balancing aesthetic, functional, and experiential goals addresses the distinction between useful art and fine art (B.1.a).
Painting as the application of pigment to surfaces producing artistic physical objects addresses art by reference to the manipulation of physical matter (B.1.b).
Visual arts as static or object-based art forms address classification of the arts (B) and major distinctions among kinds of art (B.1).
Literature as written and oral works using notational devices to create artistic expression addresses art by reference to notational devices (B.1.d).
Theatre as collaborative performing art with actors as interpreters addresses art by reference to performers as interpreters of works (B.1.c).
Opera as musical theatre with performers interpreting dramatic roles addresses art by performers as interpreters (B.1.c) and the use of musical notation (B.1.d).
Dance as body movement performed for audiences addresses art by performers as creators (B.1.c) and constitutes a time art (B.2).
Performing arts as live audience arts address art by performers as interpreters or creators (B.1.c) and the time arts distinction (B.2).
Folk music transmitted orally and associated with regional cultural identity addresses the primitive, folk, and popular arts (B.3).
Jewellery as decorative items serving aesthetic, status, and ritual functions addresses art that is both useful and fine (B.1.a) and the manipulation of physical matter (B.1.b).
Freud's theories of the unconscious, dream interpretation, and cultural analysis connect to expressive theories of art (A.2) and the creative process (A.3).
Emotion as neurophysiological states associated with experience connects to expressive theories of art concerning the functions and effects of art (A.2).

Ancient Greece's philosophical foundations including Plato's and Aristotle's aesthetics address theories of art (A.2), while its distinctive art and architecture exemplify cultural characterization (B.3).
